meta.name: ACE Adverse Childhood Experiences Questionnaire
meta.description: The ACE Questionnaire is a 10-item screening tool that assesses adverse childhood experiences before age 18. It covers abuse (physical, emotional, sexual), neglect (physical, emotional), and household dysfunction (domestic violence, substance abuse, mental illness, parental separation, incarceration). Higher ACE scores are associated with increased risk of health problems in adulthood.
meta.author: Felitti et al. (1998)
meta.reference: Felitti VJ, Anda RF, Nordenberg D, et al. Relationship of childhood abuse and household dysfunction to many of the leading causes of death in adults: the Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACE) Study. Am J Prev Med. 1998;14(4):245-258.
meta.instruction: Prior to your 18th birthday, did any of the following happen to you? Please answer each question honestly.

questions.text: Did a parent or other adult in the household often swear at you, insult you, put you down, or humiliate you, or act in a way that made you afraid that you might be physically hurt?
Did a parent or other adult in the household often push, grab, slap, or throw something at you, or ever hit you so hard that you had marks or were injured?
Did an adult or person at least 5 years older than you ever touch or fondle you or have you touch their body in a sexual way, or attempt or actually have oral, anal, or vaginal intercourse with you?
Did you often feel that no one in your family loved you or thought you were important or special, or your family didn't look out for each other, feel close to each other, or support each other?

Did you often feel that you didn't have enough to eat, had to wear dirty clothes, and had no one to protect you, or your parents were too drunk or high to take care of you or take you to the doctor if you needed it?
Were your parents ever separated or divorced?

Was your mother or stepmother often pushed, grabbed, slapped, or had something thrown at her, or sometimes or often kicked, bitten, hit with a fist, or hit with something hard, or ever repeatedly hit or threatened with a knife or gun?
Did you live with anyone who was a problem drinker or alcoholic, or who used street drugs?
Was a household member depressed or mentally ill, or did a household member attempt suicide?
Did a household member go to prison?

ranges.label: No Adverse Experiences
ranges.description: You reported no adverse childhood experiences. The absence of early adversity is a significant protective factor for your long-term physical and mental health outcomes across the lifespan.
suggestions: Continue nurturing healthy, supportive relationships in your life
If you care for children, strive to maintain safe and stable environments for them
Consider learning about ACEs to support others who may have experienced childhood adversity

ranges.label: Low Risk
ranges.description: You reported one adverse childhood experience. Even a single ACE can have subtle effects on health and coping patterns. Awareness of this history can help you take proactive steps toward resilience.
suggestions: Learn how childhood experiences can shape adult health behaviors and stress responses
Develop and maintain healthy coping strategies such as exercise, social connection, and mindfulness
Build a strong social support network of trusted friends and family members
Consider speaking with a counselor if past experiences still affect your daily life

ranges.label: Moderate Risk
ranges.description: You reported 2 to 3 adverse childhood experiences. Research shows the cumulative burden of multiple ACEs can increase vulnerability to chronic health conditions, mental health challenges, and maladaptive coping.
suggestions: Prioritize your mental health by scheduling regular self-check-ins and wellness activities
Learn evidence-based stress management techniques such as deep breathing and progressive relaxation
Consider seeking professional counseling to process past experiences in a safe setting
Invest in building a reliable social support network to buffer against life stressors
Practice self-compassion and recognize that healing from childhood adversity is a gradual process

ranges.label: High Risk
ranges.description: You reported 4 or more adverse childhood experiences. A high ACE score is strongly linked to elevated risk for depression, substance use, cardiovascular disease, and other serious health outcomes.
suggestions: Seeking professional psychological support is strongly recommended to address the impact of multiple ACEs
Trauma-informed therapies such as EMDR or trauma-focused CBT can be highly effective for healing
Monitor both your physical and mental health closely and attend regular medical check-ups
Share your experiences with trusted people so you do not carry the burden alone
Remember that ACE scores are not destiny — with appropriate support, recovery is achievable
